coredata.dc:title: Construction of hydrophobic interface on natural biomaterials for higher efficient and reversible radioactive iodine adsorption in water
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Baozhan
Liu, Xiaoxia
Hu, Jing
Wang, Fengyi
Hu, Xuan
Zhu, Yue
Lv, Xu
Du, Juan
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: For the pollution of radioactive materials, it is of great importance to develop efficient adsorbents for radioactive iodine adsorption in aqueous solution. In this work, a simple and green strategy was developed to construct hydrophobic surface on natural cotton fibers (n-CF) based on organic-soluble carbon dots (OCDs) for the first time. The results demonstrated the successful constructed hydrophobic n-CF@OCDs expressed excellent stability and selectivity for iodine (I2) adsorption in water. The maximum adsorption capacity for I2 on n-CF@OCDs is calculated to be 190.1 mg g−1, which is about 6.8 times higher than that of n-CF (28.1 mg g−1), this highly I2 adsorption efficiency should be attributed to the hydrophobic properties of adsorbent. The adsorption mechanism was also discussed in this work. In addition, the adsorbed I2 could be desorbed easily with a simple reductive process at ambient conditions, which can lead to not only the restore of I2 but also the recycling of adsorbent, illustrating their good practicability. Furthermore, this universal strategy can also be used for construction of hydrophobic surface on various natural biomaterials, demonstrating its potential application in constructing of hydrophobic surface and used for the adsorption and removal of nonpolar pollutions or radioactive waste in aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophobic surface
Organic-soluble carbon dots
Natural biomaterials
Adsorption
Radioactive iodine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of OCDs modified natural cotton fiber (n-CF@OCDs)
Cycling stability of n-CF@OCDs and construction of other adsorbents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the transformation, adsorption and desorption of I2 on the surface of n-CF@OCDs.

(A) A schematic illustration of simple preparation of n-CF@OCDs. (B,C) The SEM images of n-CF and n-CF@OCDs, respectively. The insets were the high resolution images of one fibre. (D,E) The TEM images of n-CF and n-CF@OCDs, respectively.

(A) XPS survey spectra of n-CF (a) and n-CF@OCDs (b); (B) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s; (C) The FT-IR spectra of n-CF@OCDs (a), n-CF (b) and OCDs (c); (D) The contact angle of n-CF@OCDs after the modification of OCDs.
The bright-field (a) and 3D fluorescent (b) images of n-CF (A) and n-CF@OCDs (B), respectively. The scale bar was 100 μm.

Kinetics of I2 adsorption on n-CF and n-CF@OCDs adsorbents. (A) Adsorption amounts of I2 on n-CF and n-CF@OCDs as a function of time. Error bars represent standard deviation of triplicate measurements with 95% confidence intervals. (B) Plots of t/qt versus t for the adsorption of I2 [data from (A)].

(A) Isotherm adsorption of iodine on n-CF and n-CF@OCDs adsorbents. Error bars represent standard deviation of triplicate measurements with 95% confidence intervals. B) The corresponding plots of Ce/qe versus Ce for n-CF and n-CF@OCDs, respectively [data from (A)].

(A) Bar plots showing the adsorption of iodine for 5 cycles using n-CF@OCDs as adsorbent. (B) The removal efficiency for iodine based on different natural materials (a, grass; b. rice husk; c. corncob; d. wood dust; e. ginkgo leaf; f. cotton) before (black bar) and after (red bar) the modification of OCDs (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Construction of hydrophobic interface on natural biomaterials for higher efficient and reversible radioactive iodine adsorption in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Wangjiang Road, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Wangjiang Road, Chengdu 610064, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the pollution of radioactive materials, it is of great importance to develop efficient adsorbents for radioactive iodine adsorption in aqueous solution. In this work, a simple and green strategy was developed to construct hydrophobic surface on natural cotton fibers (n-CF) based on organic-soluble carbon dots (OCDs) for the first time. The results demonstrated the successful constructed hydrophobic n-CF@OCDs expressed excellent stability and selectivity for iodine (I2) adsorption in water. The maximum adsorption capacity for I2 on n-CF@OCDs is calculated to be 190.1 mg g−1, which is about 6.8 times higher than that of n-CF (28.1 mg g−1), this highly I2 adsorption efficiency should be attributed to the hydrophobic properties of adsorbent. The adsorption mechanism was also discussed in this work. In addition, the adsorbed I2 could be desorbed easily with a simple reductive process at ambient conditions, which can lead to not only the restore of I2 but also the recycling of adsorbent, illustrating their good practicability. Furthermore, this universal strategy can also be used for construction of hydrophobic surface on various natural biomaterials, demonstrating its potential application in constructing of hydrophobic surface and used for the adsorption and removal of nonpolar pollutions or radioactive waste in aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the extensive application of nuclear energy, a large number of radioactive waste have been released into the environments, which can bring serious threaten to livings within the ecosystem [1]. Therefore, the environmental and health issues caused by nuclear waste have drawn the increasing attention, especially from the Chernobyl Disaster in 1986 and Fukushima nuclear disaster in 2011 [2,3], and radioactive iodine (RI, including 129I and 131I) was one of the major components of the radionuclides [3]. For example, 129I is an inevitable by-product of nuclear fission with a long-lived half-life of 1.57 × 107 years [4], its present in environment can bring serious threaten to public, so it must be captured and reliably stored while it decays. Whereas 131I is a short-lived half-life isotope (about 8.02 days) [5], which has been widely used in radiation therapy and biomedical studies for various thyroid diseases [6]; so the disposal of radioactive iodine from numerous medical research institutions is also considerable, and its discharging into our circumstances can directly affect the human metabolic process. Therefore, it is agency to develop a fast and efficient method for the capture and recycling of radioactive iodine from environment.

Until to now, various strategies have been developed for the efficient capture of radioactive iodine from aqueous media, and these reported methods can be mainly divided into two categories. One is the chemical adsorption and the other is the physical adsorption. The chemical adsorption mainly based on the chemical reaction between metal and iodine ions. For instance, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu+, are usually used to interact with I− to form precipitation [7–11], which could be removed from water. This is an efficient process and suitable for capturing trace iodine ions from large volumes of aqueous solution. However, the disadvantages of high cost of metal (especially for Au or Ag) and the serious environmental issues caused by heavy metal ion leaching limits their extensive use. In addition, the lower desorption efficiency and unsatisfactory recyclability of these materials are also the limitations due to the strong binds between metal and iodine ions. For the physical adsorption, a hydrophobic interface of adsorbent is needed and plays very important roles for iodine adsorbing in aqueous solution [12]. Based on this, series of materials, including inorganic porous materials [13], activated carbons [14], zeolites materials [9,12], metal organic frameworks (MOF) [15] and ionic liquids [16] have been synthesized and selected as the adsorbent for I2 adsorption. Among these materials, MOFs have been widely selected as the adsorbent to investigate their I2 capturing properties [17,18]. However, the complicated synthesis procedure and low stability in acidic conditions of MOFs are the limitations for their practical applications [19]. Furthermore, the low capturing efficiency of MOFs also restricts their practical application because their electron-rich organic ligands could reduce I2 into I− in aqueous solution, the usually used active carbon for I2 caption also contains this electron-rich organic functional groups [12]. Comparing with these two kinds of I2-capturing method, the physical adsorption is much simpler and economical, and has potential application for the I2-capturing in aqueous solution. But due to the above disadvantages, it is necessary and agency to develop a new strategy to simply construct hydrophobic surface of adsorbents with excellent stability and higher adsorption capacity to iodine adsorption and recovery.

As a newcomer of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) recently attracted tremendous attention, and have a widely applications in bioimaging, sensors, photocatalysis and solar cells due to their promising properties of promising photoluminescence, excellent physical-chemical stability, nontoxicity, low-cost, biocompatibility, and so on [20–24]. However, most of the carbon dots reported are hydrophilic in nature, and there are few reports about the hydrophobic carbon dots [25,26], especially for their application studying. In our previous work, a controllable method was developed to synthesis hydrophobic carbon dots at ambient conditions [27], and the excellent dispersibility in organic solvents indicates its good hydrophobic property. Therefore, we predict that this organic soluble carbon dots can be used for constructing hydrophobic surface easily, which will have a positive effect for the adsorption of radioactive iodine in aqueous solution.

Herein, a strategy for construction of hydrophobic surface on adsorbent was developed only by simply adsorbing organic-soluble carbon dots (OCDs) on natural cotton fibers (n-CF). The central idea lies in the hydrophobic property of OCDs, which could not only lead to the formation of hydrophobic surface, but also lead to the higher stability in water due to its excellent hydrophobic properties. Using as an adsorbent, the constructed n-CF@OCDs shows quick and higher selective adsorption for I2 in water, and the adsorption efficiency of which to I2 has been greatly enhanced comparing with that of n-CF without OCDs modification. In addition, the adsorbed I2 could also be desorbed by a simple chemical reduction process, and the results indicate the excellent repeatability of n-CF@OCDs. Notably, this is a universal strategy, and could be used for construction hydrophobic surface on large number of materials. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report about the constructing of hydrophobic surface on natural materials by directly using OCDs instead of traditional surfactant. We anticipate that this work will open an avenue for the adsorption and remove of nonpolar pollutions in environment.

Cetylpyridinium chloride monohydrate (CPC) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Anhydrous sodium sulphite (Na2SO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals (Chengdu, China), and a fresh solution of H2O2 was prepared daily. Sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium iodate (KIO3), potassium iodide (KI) and petroleum ether were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The fibrous natural cotton, grass, rice husk, wood dust and ginkgo leaf were purchased at local supermarket (Chengdu, China) and used directly without any further treatment. All reagents are of analytical grade and used as received. Nanopure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was from a Millipore filtration system and used throughout the experiments.

The OCDs were synthesized by simply mixing of CPC with NaOH aqueous solution at room temperature according to our previous work [27]. In a typical synthesis, 10.0 mL NaOH (2.0 mol L−1) was added into a CPC aqueous solution (1 L, 0.1 mol L−1) and left to stand for 12 h at room temperature. The color of solution changes from colourless to light yellow and then to wine red as the reaction time proceeded, indicating the carbonisation of CPC, and the precipitate in aqueous solution proves the formation of OCDs. After that, 100 mL petroleum ether was added into the above aqueous solution, and OCDs could be transferred into the organic phase quickly due to its excellent hydrophobicity. After separating off the aqueous phase, the OCDs powder could be obtained by evaporating solvent with a vacuum drying at 60 °C, and the OCDs ethanol solutions with different concentration were prepared by dispersing different quality of OCDs in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OCDs modified natural cotton fiber (n-CF@OCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The n-CF@OCDs was prepared through a simple physical adsorption process. Typically, a weighted n-CF was immersed into the above OCDs ethanol solution with different concentration and gently stirred for 2 h at room temperature. The color of the n-CF changed from white to brown, indicating the successful modification of OCDs on its surface. The ethanol solution was then separated from cotton by centrifuging at 2000 rpm for 5 min. After washing with water for three times, the n-CF@OCDs with different quality of OCDs loading could be obtained by vacuum drying at 60 °C for 2 h.

The morphologies of n-CF and n-CF@OCDs were investigated on a Hitachi S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were carried out to examine the microstructure of the n-CF and n-CF@OCDs by using a JEOL (JEM-2100) field emission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV equipped with an energy-dispersive spectrometer. The XPS spectra were measured on a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscope using Mg as the excitation source (Kratos, UK). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectra of n-CF and n-CF@OCDs was acquired using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA). Raman spectroscopy measurements were carried out on a HORIBA R-XploRA Plus instrument with a 532 nm excitation wavelength. The wavenumber values were measured over the range 90-250 cm−1 with an average of three scans. Confocal fluorescence images were performed by a confocal laser scanning microscopy (Nikon, N-SIM). UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Hitachi U-2900 Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) with a 1 cm quartz cuvette. Water contact-angle measurements were performed on JC200D2H contact angle meter (Zhongchen digital equipment Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China) at room temperature.

Because the n-CF@OCDs have a selective adsorption to iodine molecular (I2), but the iodine mainly exists in the form of IO3 − and/or I−, therefore, a translation process of IO3 − and/or I− to I2 should be included before the adsorption. In a typical experiment, Na2SO3 was added into IO3 −-contained solution (400 ppm, 50 mL), and the pH was then adjusted to 1.0 with HCl (1:1, v/v), with this procedure, the IO3 − ions in the solution could be reduced to I− by Na2SO3 (Eq. (1)). Two minutes later, excess H2O2 (30%) was added, and the formed or original I− in the solution could then be oxidized to I2 by H2O2 (Eq. (2)). Therefore, both of IO3 − and I− in the solution could all be transferred to I2 with this reduction-oxidation process. The final volume was then adjusted to 100 mL with distilled water. After that, 0.1 g as-prepared adsorbent was immersed into the I2-contained solution and the mixture was gently shaken at room temperature. In the process of adsorption, 0.5 mL of the reaction solution was taken out at set intervals, and measured with UV–vis absorption spectrometer by monitoring the absorbance change at a wavelength of 400 nm. In order to obtain an accurate concentration of I2, each sample was measured at least three times.
(1)
I
O 3 -
+ 3 S
O 3
2 -
=
I -
+ 3 S
O 4
2 -
(2)
2
I −
+
H 2
O 2
+ 2
H +
=
I 2
+ 2
H 2
O
By measuring the residual concentration of I2 in the solution, the corresponding I2 absorbed on adsorbent could be calculated; the iodine adsorption amount (qe , Eq. (3)) and removal efficiency (R, Eq. (4)) were calculated by the following equations:
(3)
q e
=
( C 0 − C e ) V
W
(4)
R ( % ) = ( 1 −
C e
C 0
) × 100 %
where qe is the amount of the iodine adsorbed on the adsorbent at equilibrium time (mg g−1), V the solution volume (L) and W the mass of the adsorbent (g). C0 and Ce are the initial and equilibrium iodine contents (mg L−1) in the solution, respectively.

After the adsorption, the adsorbent was separated from the solution and then regenerated by dipping into another aqueous solution (pH = 1) with excess Na2SO3 contained. In this process, the adsorbed I2 could be reduced to I− and desorbed from n-CF@OCDs, which was then washed with water for three times. Recycling tests were carried out through this adsorption-desorption process using the same adsorbent, and the adsorbed amount of iodine was determined after each cycle.

The hydrophobic adsorbent (n-CF@OCDs) was designed and constructed by directly loading of as-prepared OCDs on the surface of natural cotton fiber (n-CF) (Fig. 1 A); this is a simple physical adsorption procedure and carried out at ambient conditions. Therefore, it is a green and convenient strategy for construction of hydrophobic adsorbent, and could be used for preparation of n-CF@OCDs on large scale. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was firstly used to characterize the morphologies of n-CF before (Fig. 1B) and after the modification of OCDs (Fig. 1C), respectively. It can be seen that the n-CF posses a 1D structure and has a smooth surface (inset of Fig. 1B). After the modification with OCDs (Fig. 1C), its 1D morphology structure do not significantly influenced, but the OCDs was not obviously observed on the surface of n-CF due to their ultra-small size (Fig. S1). While from the enlarged view (inset of Fig. 1C), we can see the surface of n-CF@OCDs becomes more rough compared to that of n-CF (inset of Fig. 1B). In order to demonstrate the presence of OCDs, the n-CF and n-CF@OCDs were characterized with the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) respectively. As shown in Fig. 1E, OCDs were obviously present and randomly distributed over the entire n-CF surface after the modification. By contrast, there were no OCDs observed on the surface of n-CF (Fig. 1D). These results clearly indicate the successful modification of OCDs on the surface of n-CF with this physical adsorption technology.

The compositions of constructed n-CF@OCDs and n-CF were then measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Form the survey spectra of n-CF before and after modification of OCDs shown in Fig. 2 A, it can be seen that there are two predominant peaks at 284.0 and 530.6 eV for n-CF, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s and O1s respectively [28]. However, a third peak at around 400 eV, corresponding to N1s [29], was present in the spectrum of n-CF@OCDs, which should be come from the synthesized OCDs. The high-resolution XPS of N1s shown in Fig. 2B was decomposed into two peaks at 399.2 and 400.5 eV, which should be assigned to graphite-like N, pyridine-like N and NH group, respectively [30]. The present of N element clearly indicate the present of OCDs on the surface of n-CF, because there was no N element contained in n-CF. To further confirm the chemical groups’ changes on n-CF after the modification of OCDs, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of n-CF, OCDs and n-CF@OCDs were also characterized. As shown in Fig. 2C, the broad band between 3100-3700 cm−1 for OCDs, n-CF and n-CF@OCDs should be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, and the bands at 1650 cm−1 was the CC stretching from sp2 carbon network [31,32]. The n-CF@OCDs displays a prominent band at 2924 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1, which should be attributed to the CH stretching vibration from −CH2– and −CH3 [33]. The peak at about 721 cm−1 should come from the aliphatic carbon chains −(CH2)n−CH3 (n≤15) on the surface of OCDs, which would play a very important role in the construction of hydrophobic surface of n-CF [27]. The obvious increasing of peaks at 2924 cm−1, 2850 cm−1 and 721 cm−1 for n-CF@OCDs compared with that of n-CF clearly indicates the successful modification of OCDs on the surface of n-CF with this simple adsorption strategy, which is in a good agreement with the results from above mentioned. Meanwhile, the hydrophobicity of n-CF before (n-CF) and after the modification of OCDs (n-CF@OCDs) was investigated by measure their contact angle. The result shown in Fig. 2D indicates that the contact angle of n-CF@OCDs is 145° after the modification of OCDs, which is greatly enhanced than that of n-CF (126°) without OCDs modification (Fig. S2). This result demonstrates the successful construction of hydrophobic surface of n-CF with OCDs.

To further demonstrate the successful modification of n-CF with OCDs, the fluorescence properties of n-CF before and after loading of OCDs were also investigated. Fig. 3 presents the photographs of n-CF (A) and n-CF@OCDs (B) with the irradiation of day light (a) and UV light (365 nm) (b), respectively. It can be seen that there is an obvious fluorescent difference after the modification. For n-CF, only weak blue fluorescent emission was observed under the irradiation of UV light (Fig. 3Ab), while a much stronger green fluorescence from n-CF@OCDs (Fig. 3Bb) was present at the same conditions. The stronger and uniform green fluorescence from n-CF@OCDs (Fig. 3Bb) compared to that of n-CF (Fig. 3Ab) not only demonstrates the successfully constructed n-CF@OCDs with uniform distribution, but also indicates the excellent stability of OCDs on n-CF, which could not be washed off by water for several times, indicating their potential applications in aqueous media.

In this work, we suspected that the constructed hydrophobic interface may help to adsorption nonpolar molecular on its surface based on the similar dissolve mutually theory. Therefore, the adsorption of I2 in aqueous solution was investigated by using n-CF and n-CF@OCDs as adsorbent, respectively. However, considering the safety of the laboratory, non-radioactive iodide was used in this work to investigate the adsorption performance of adsorbents, which could also provide good information about the adsorption of radioactive iodide. It is well known that the iodine mainly exists as the form of IO3 − and I−, and they could not be adsorbed efficiently by hydrophobic surface due to their excellent solubility in aqueous solution. Therefore, the IO3 − and/or I− ions should be transformed into I2 with the previous discussed chemical process using Na2SO3 and H2O2 as reducing and oxidizing agents, respectively.

To establish the optimal conditions for I2 adsorption on n-CF@OCDs, several conditions, including the effects of pH, adsorption time, amounts of OCDs on n-CF, were investigated in this work. From Fig. S3A, it can be seen that the adsorption efficiency of n-CF@OCDs to I2 decreases as the increasing pH of the solution, which should be attributed to that the Na2SO3 has strong reducibility under acidic conditions, and IO3 − could be transformed to I- completely at this condition. In addition, the acid condition is helpful for the oxidation of I− by H2O2 and the formed I2 is stable under this conditions. Therefore, the experiments for I2 absorption were carried out at pH = 1.0. The hydrophobicity of n-CF@OCDs and the nonpolar property of I2 make this adsorption system possess excellent selectivity. In order to demonstrate this, the adsorption of I2 on n-CF@OCDs was investigated in 100 mL iodine contained solution (200 ppm) with the presence of Cl−, NO3- and SO4 2− anions (2 g L-1). As shown in Fig. S3B, the results imply that the presence of Cl−, SO4 2−, Mg2+, Na+ and Ca2+ do not affect the adsorption of I2, whereas the presence of NO3 − could slightly hinder the adsorption. It can be interpreted that the NO3 − are ascribed to strong acid anions, its strong oxidability at acid condition can oxidize the formed I2 to IO3-, which cannot be adsorbed by n-CF@OCDs due to its excellent water solubility.

For I2 adsorption, the present of OCDs on n-CF@OCDs plays an important role; therefore, the adsorption of I2 on n-CF@OCDs with different OCDs loading was also investigated. Fig. S4 shows the dependence of contact time on the adsorption amount of I2 in aqueous solution by n-CF and n-CF@ODs, respectively. It can be seen that the adsorption of I2 on n-CF@OCDs was very rapid in the first 10 min, and then gradually increased as the contact time was prolonged. After 20 min of adsorption, the amount of I2 remains constant, this suggests that 20 min is the equilibrium time for this adsorption process. In addition, the adsorption capacity of n-CF@OCDs to I2 increases with the increasing loading quantity of OCDs, and the amount of adsorbed I2 was about 20 mg g−1 for n-CF and 118 mg g−1 for n-CF@OCDs with a OCDs loading of 232 mg g−1. This should be ascribed to the specific surface of n-CF@OCDs after the loading of OCDs, which can greatly enhance the adsorption capacity of I2 in aqueous solution.

Above descriptions have revealed that the constructed hydrophobic materials of n-CF@OCDs posses excellent adsorption properties, and could be used as a good adsorbent for I2 adsorption in aqueous solution. Fig. 4 A shows the adsorption rates of I2 on n-CF and n-CF@OCDs with OCDs loading amounts of 232 mg g−1, respectively. The results indicate that the adsorption capacity increases with the increasing contact time, and the equilibrium could be reached within 20 min on both materials. It can also be seen from the results that both of the n-CF and n-CF@OCDs all can absorb I2 in aqueous, and the adsorption capacity of n-CF@OCDs to I2 was enhanced greatly than that of n-CF. The kinetic behaviours of I2 adsorption shown in Fig. 4B can be well described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and expressed as follows:
t
q t
=
1
k
q e 2
+
t
q e
where qt and qe represent the amounts of I2 (mg g−1)adsorbed at time t and at equilibrium, respectively; k is the adsorption kinetic rate constant. The result shown in Fig. 4B indicates the t/qt versus t is a straight-line, and the linear correlation coefficients r is 0.9998 and 0.9945 for n-CF and n-CF@OCDs, respectively.

To thoroughly understand the adsorption process, the adsorption isotherms for I2 on n-CF and n-CF@OCDs were investigated respectively. Fig. 5 A presents the adsorption isotherms of I2 on the n-CF and n-CF@OCDs (with a OCDs loading amounts of 232 mg g−1) adsorbents by varying initial iodine concentrations. The results indicate that the adsorption capacities on both adsorbents increased with the increasing equilibrium concentration of I2. The classic Langmuir adsorption model was used to describe the adsorption equilibrium as below:
C e
q e
=
1
k
q m
+
C e
q m
where qm (mg g−1) is the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of I2 corresponding to complete monolayer coverage, k is the adsorption constant of the Langmuir models. The results as depicted in Fig. 5B indicate that the Langmuir isotherm was linear over the entire concentration range with high coefficients (r = 0.9984 and 0.9970) for n-CF and n-CF@OCDs respectively, which suggests the Langmuir isotherm is a good model of this sorption system. From the fitting curves of the Langmuir model (Fig. 5B), the ideal maximum adsorption capacity (qm ) for I2 on n-CF@OCDs was determined to be 190.1 mg g-1, which is about 6.8 times higher than that of n-CF without modification (28.1 mg g-1). This also indicates the adsorption capacity of I2 on n-CF@OCDs was greatly enhanced after the modification of OCDs compared to that of n-CF. This result also indicates that the OCDs loading on n-CF surface play key roles in the adsorption of I2 in aqueous solution, and the I2 adsorption capacity on OCDs could be calculated to be 861.7 mg g−1(OCDs), which is not the highest compared to previous reported adsorbents (Table S1) [4,7,10,34–47], but the advantages of eco-friendly, low-cost, simple construction, shorter equilibrium time, especially the excellent stability in aqueous solution, will make this adsorbent have widely potential practical applications.

Iodine (I2), a nonpolar molecular, has a much higher solubility in nonpolar solvents (such as hexane and carbon tetrachloride) than that in aqueous solution. Therefore, construction a hydrophobic surface on adsorbents is in favor of the adsorption of iodine in aqueous solution. We infer that there are two main steps in this adsorption process: one is the transformation of I2 from IO3 − and/or I−. We know that iodine mainly exists in environment as ion states (IO3 − and/or I−), which could not be adsorbed on the surface of n-CF@OCDs. In order to obtain higher adsorption efficiency, these ions should be transferred into I2 using SO3 2- and H2O2 as reductive and oxidative agent respectively. After the formation of I2, the color of the solution changes from colorless to yellowish-brown (Scheme 1 A–B). With the presence of n-CF@OCDs in this I2-contained solution, the formed I2 could be immigrated to the surface of adsorbent quickly based on its nonpolar property and the hydrophobic property of n-CF@OCDs (Scheme 1B-C). This is the other step for the adsorption of I2 on n-CF@OCDs. In this procedure, the solution gradually change from yellowish-brown in the initial stage to colorless at the equilibrium, which can demonstrate the migration of I2 from aqueous phase to the surface of n-CF@OCDs. To further demonstrate this result, the Raman spectroscopy was used for the detection of iodine on adsorbent before (n-CF@OCDs) and after I2 adsorption (n-CF@OCDs-I2). From the result shown in Fig. S5, it can be seen that there is no obvious Raman scattering observed for n-CF@OCDs, indicates there is no iodine present on n-CF@OCDs. But for n-CF@OCDs-I2, the obvious Raman peak at 167.9 cm-1, which has been demonstrated to be originated from (I2)n by previous work [48], indicates the adsorbed iodine exist on n-CF@OCDs surface mainly as a form of (I2)n. In addition, the not obvious peak present at about 113 cm-1 (attributed to I3 −) proves that there is few I3 − on the n-CF@OCDs-I2, which should be attributed to the excess H2O2 present in the system. Therefore, we can conclude that the iodine adsorbed on the surface of n-CF@OCDs mainly exist as a form of (I2)n, and the adsorption of I2 on n-CF@OCDs is based on the similar dissolve mutually theory, in which the design and construction of hydrophobic surface of adsorbents by OCDs is important and plays very important roles in I2 adsorption with higher adsorption efficiency. In addition, the excellent selectivity of adsorbent is also attributed to the presence of OCDs, because it can prevent the adsorption of common ions for their excellent water-soluble properties. After the adsorption, the n-CF@OCDs with adsorbed (I2)n was then dipped into another aqueous solution with Na2SO3 contained, which can reduce (I2)n to I−, and the generated I− can migrate quickly into the aqueous solution again from the surface of n-CF@OCDs due to its excellent water soluble property (Scheme 1C-D). Therefore, the reduction of (I2)n to I− in this desorption procedure can not only lead to the concentration of iodine, which is beneficial for further treatment, but also solve the difficulty of long time storage of iodine due to its highly volatile. At the same time, the I2 desorption from adsorbents in this procedure could also achieve the reusability of n-CF@OCDs for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cycling stability of n-CF@OCDs and construction of other adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The cycling stability of adsorbent plays an important role and is a bottleneck for practical application in the irradiative iodine adsorption. Therefore, the cycling stability of n-CF@OCDs was investigated under the same conditions. After desorption of I2 as described above, the n-CF@OCDs was then used for the second adsorption of I2 again, and these desorption and adsorption process was repeated for several times. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the recycling tests of n-CF@OCDs adsorbents indicate that the adsorption amount of iodine at equilibrium have a slightly decrease in the first two cycles, and remain nearly unchanged after the third cycles using, this demonstrates the excellent cycling stability of n-CF@OCDs, which should be attributed to the hydrophobic property of OCDs. This easy separation and recycling, excellent reusability of n-CF@OCDs suggest its great potential for practical application. For the practical application, this strategy is more suitable for the adsorption and removal of iodine in the pollutions from hospital or nuclear research institutions, in which comparable small volume but with higher concentration of radiate iodine would usually be produced. Before the adsorption, the pollutions were firstly reduced with Na2SO3 and then oxidized by H2O2 at acid condition, and the different species of iodine could be transferred to I2, which can be effectively adsorbed on the n-CF@OCDs surface. Thus, the radiate iodine could be removed and the n-CF@OCDs could be reused with a simple desorption process as described above.

In addition, this strategy is universal and could be used for constructing hydrophobic surface on many kinds of materials. In order to demonstrate this, several common natural materials, except the natural cotton described above, were selected to be modified with OCDs to construct hydrophobic surface and used for the adsorption of iodine in aqueous solution. Fig. 6B compares the adsorption properties of different adsorbents, including grass, rice husk, wood dust and ginkgo leaf, for I2 adsorption before and after the modification of OCDs. It can be seen that all these materials can be used for the adsorption of I2 and the adsorption efficiency was greatly enhanced after the modification of OCDs, which is attributed to the formation of hydrophobic surface. This result also demonstrates that this is a simple and universal strategy, which could be used for construction hydrophobic surface on different materials.

In summary, a feasible and simple strategy has been developed to construct hydrophobic surface by directly modification of OCDs on natural cotton fibers (n-CF). The results demonstrate that the OCDs have been successfully modified on n-CF, and the constructed n-CF@OCDs exhibits excellent stability in water and have been used for the adsorption of I2 in aqueous solution. The adsorption results proved that the adsorption capacity of n-CF@OCDs to I2 was enhanced largely after the modification of OCDs, which is about 6.8 times higher than that of n-CF without OCDs modification. The adsorption mechanism for I2 was also discussed, and demonstrating the enhanced adsorption efficiency should be attributed to the constructed hydrophobic surface after the modification of OCDs, which plays a very important role in I2 adsorption. In addition, the adsorbed I2 could be desorbed simply with a simple reduction process, which ensures the excellent cycling stability of n-CF@OCDs for practical application. More importantly, this is a universal method, and has been successfully used for the construction of hydrophobic surface on many natural materials. Given the widely application of hydrophobic adsorbent, we suggest that our approach with mild construction conditions will shine a new light on the problem of construction of hydrophobic adsorbents and further expand their properties for specific applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. U1833124, 21876117 and U1833202). Prof. Peng Wu in Analytical & Testing Center at Sichuan University was greatly appreciated for the measures of n-CF and n-CF@OCDs fluorescent images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.01.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
